title: Cathead biscuits
subtitle: Southern US drop biscuits, generous and soft
excerpt: Large Southern-style buttermilk biscuits dropped from a spoon and baked until just golden. No rolling, no cutter. Tall, tender, and soft inside with a rough, uneven top. Makes eight large biscuits.
sourceNotes: The cathead biscuit is a Southern US regional tradition named for the size of the finished biscuit (as large as a cat's head). No single public-domain text documents the recipe in its current drop-biscuit form. The technique draws on Southern household cooking practice documented in the USDA National Agricultural Library collections and American food-heritage archives, where large handmade drop biscuits appear in Depression-era and wartime home-cooking guidance.

content.text: Cathead biscuits are named for their size: as large and unruly as a cat's head. Where the British scone is pressed and cut, the cathead is torn from the bowl and dropped straight onto the tray. The rough surface is not a flaw. It catches the heat and crisps while the inside stays soft and layered.

Lard is traditional. Cold unsalted butter works well and gives a clean flavour. Buttermilk is not negotiable: its acidity reacts with the raising agent and produces the characteristic open, tender crumb. Whole milk with a teaspoon of lemon juice, left to stand for five minutes, is a workable substitute.
Preheat the oven to 220°C fan. Line a large baking tray with baking paper. Whisk together the

and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a large bowl. Rub in the cold {{unsalted-butter}} with your fingertips until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs with a few pea-sized pieces still visible. Those pieces are what make the biscuit flaky.

Pour in the cold {{buttermilk}} and stir with a fork just until a shaggy dough comes together. The dough should look rough and sticky. Do not knead. Overworking develops gluten and toughens the crumb.

Using two large spoons or an ice-cream scoop, drop eight mounds onto the lined tray, spacing them about 5 cm apart. Each mound should be roughly the size of a generous golf ball, about 70 g. Do not press them smooth.
Bake for 13 to 15 minutes, until the tops are deep golden and the biscuits have risen well. Tap the base of one: it should sound hollow. Brush with melted butter if you like, straight from the oven.
Eat warm, split horizontally. Classically with butter and honey, or with a fried egg, streaky bacon, and hot sauce. They are at their best within an hour of baking; after that the crust softens.
If the biscuits spread rather than rise, the butter was too warm or the oven was not hot enough. Use butter straight from the fridge and let the oven reach full temperature before loading the tray.
